stem: How does zero-noise extrapolation (ZNE) produce a mitigated expectation value?

options.text: It runs the circuit at several amplified noise levels, then extrapolates the results back to the zero-noise limit.
It learns the readout-error transfer matrix from calibration circuits and inverts it.
It inserts pulse sequences on idle qubits to cancel decoherence during execution.
It expresses the ideal circuit as a quasi-probability combination of noisy circuits and samples from it.

explanation.correct: ZNE has two stages: noise amplification (running the circuit at several increased noise rates, e.g. by digital gate folding) and extrapolation (fitting those noisy expectation values and extrapolating to the zero-noise limit). It often improves results but is not guaranteed to be unbiased.

distractors.B: Learning and inverting the readout-error matrix describes TREX (measurement mitigation), not ZNE.
distractors.C: Inserting pulses on idle qubits is dynamical decoupling — an error-suppression technique, not ZNE.
distractors.D: Expressing the ideal circuit as a quasi-probability combination of noisy circuits describes probabilistic error cancellation (PEC), not ZNE.
